inputs.userGoal: Provision a jumpbox VM for secure management, establish NSGs for each tier, and connect tiers using internal Azure Load Balancer
subGoals: Tiered topology: separate management, web, app, and data tiers via dedicated subnets and per-subnet NSGs
Secure operator access: jumpbox VM in dedicated management subnet, NSG locks RDP/SSH to a parameterized admin source CIDR
Internal east-west traffic: Standard SKU internal Load Balancer fronting the application tier
Tenant convention: prefer westus2 region per insights
Tenant convention: default to single-zone deployments per insights (opt into zones only when HA SLO requires)
Tenant convention: VMs hardened with TrustedLaunch on D-series sizes per insights
Tenant convention: Standard SKU Key Vault with 90-day soft-delete and RBAC authorization (modernizing access-policy default per insights)
insightsApplied: Westus2 and eastus are the dominant regions: across all 12,267 regional resources westus2 holds 25.9% and eastus 17.2% (with centralindia 9.2% and southeastasia 8.8% as secondary footprints), so new workloads should default to westus2 or eastus as the primary region and reserve centralindia and southeastasia as paired DR or geo-expansion targets unless the user specifies otherwise.

Zone-redundant configuration is the exception rather than the rule: of 317 resources exposing properties.zoneRedundant only 26.2% (83) are zone-redundant, indicating the user routinely deploys single-zone resources, so plans should default to single-zone SKUs and only opt into zone redundancy when the workload explicitly requires HA.

Public network access is the prevailing posture: 89.5% (1,154 of 1,289) of resources reporting properties.publicNetworkAccess have it Enabled and 92.9% (627 of 675) of network ACLs default to Allow, so plans should not assume a private-endpoint-first topology and should only introduce private endpoints, NSP, or default-Deny ACLs where the workload explicitly calls for isolation.

TLS 1.2 is the established minimum-TLS baseline: 96.1% (558 of 580) of resources reporting properties.minimumTlsVersion are pinned to TLS1_2, so all new TLS-capable resources in the plan must set minimumTlsVersion to TLS1_2 to stay consistent with the existing security baseline.

Virtual networks are flat and isolated rather than hub-spoke: 94.3% (200 of 212) of VNets declare a single address prefix, 92.9% (197 of 212) have zero peerings, and 97.2% have DDoS protection disabled, so the planner should default to standalone VNets with a single address space and only introduce hub-spoke peering, multi-prefix address spaces, or DDoS Standard when the workload explicitly requires shared connectivity or internet-facing protection.

Virtual machines standardize on Trusted Launch with general-purpose D-series sizes: 97.2% of VMs use securityType=TrustedLaunch, the top three sizes (Standard_D32ads_v5, Standard_D32ds_v6, Standard_D32as_v4) are all general-purpose D-series with 32 vCPUs, and 95.1% of OS disks set deleteOption=Detach, so new VMs should default to TrustedLaunch on a current-generation D-series size with osDisk deleteOption=Detach to preserve data on VM deletion.

Key Vaults use the Standard SKU with long soft-delete retention but legacy access policies: 97.7% of vaults are Standard SKU, 95.3% keep the 90-day softDeleteRetentionInDays, but only 39.3% have enableRbacAuthorization=True, so new vaults should be Standard with 90-day soft-delete retention and explicitly opt into RBAC authorization to modernize access control rather than perpetuating the access-policy default.

reasoning.whyChosen: Single resource group for the tiered network workload simplifies blast radius, RBAC, and lifecycle management.
alternativesConsidered: Per-tier resource groups
Hub-spoke split across two RGs
reasoning.tradeoffs: Single RG keeps operations simple but couples lifecycle of all tiers.

resources.name: log-net-westus2

reasoning.whyChosen: Centralized Log Analytics workspace to receive NSG flow logs and VM Insights, satisfying WAF Operational Excellence (observability).
alternativesConsidered: No centralized logging (rejected — violates WAF observability)
Multiple workspaces per tier (rejected — fragments queries)
reasoning.tradeoffs: PerGB2018 with 30-day retention balances cost vs visibility; extend retention if compliance requires.

resources.dependencyReasoning: Workspace must be created in the workload resource group.

reasoning.whyChosen: User-assigned managed identity attached to jumpbox and tier VMSS so they can read jumpbox admin secret from Key Vault without local credentials, per WAF Security pillar (no hardcoded secrets) and Azure best-practices.
alternativesConsidered: System-assigned identity per VM (rejected — harder to share Key Vault role assignments)
Service principal with client secret (rejected — secret rotation burden)
reasoning.tradeoffs: Sharing one identity widens blast radius slightly; mitigated by least-privilege RBAC.

reasoning.whyChosen: Stores jumpbox local-admin password and any tier secrets. Standard SKU with RBAC authorization, 90-day soft-delete and purge protection follow WAF Security and the Phase 1 Key Vault insight (modernizing the legacy access-policy default).
alternativesConsidered: Hardcoded password in template (rejected — secrets in source)
Premium SKU with HSM-backed keys (rejected — not required for password storage)
reasoning.tradeoffs: publicNetworkAccess kept Enabled to match tenant baseline (insight #3); private endpoint can be added later when isolation is explicitly required.

reasoning.whyChosen: Locks jumpbox RDP/SSH to a parameterized admin CIDR per WAF VM guide (avoid open management ports). Explicit deny-all catches misconfigured rules.
alternativesConsidered: Open RDP/SSH to internet (rejected — high attack surface)
Azure Bastion (deferred — user explicitly requested a jumpbox VM)
reasoning.tradeoffs: Operator must keep admin CIDR list current; Bastion remains the recommended hardening if a public IP is undesirable.

reasoning.whyChosen: Allows public 80/443 to the web tier, jumpbox-only management, and the mandatory AzureLoadBalancer service tag (per LB pairing constraint). Explicit deny-all enforces tier isolation.
alternativesConsidered: Single shared NSG across all tiers (rejected — violates per-tier segmentation requirement)
reasoning.tradeoffs: Standard LB requires NSG; AzureLoadBalancer service tag must remain allowed.

reasoning.whyChosen: App tier only receives traffic from the web subnet (via internal LB) on the application port, plus health probes from AzureLoadBalancer and management from jumpbox. Implements WAF Security tier segmentation.
alternativesConsidered: Allow direct internet (rejected — defeats internal LB pattern)
reasoning.tradeoffs: Application port (8080) is a placeholder; adjust per actual workload.

reasoning.whyChosen: Most-restrictive tier: only SQL traffic from app subnet plus management from jumpbox. Enforces defense-in-depth for the data tier.
alternativesConsidered: Open data tier to web (rejected — bypasses app tier)
reasoning.tradeoffs: 1433 is a placeholder; change for non-SQL workloads.

resources.name: vnet-tiered

reasoning.whyChosen: Single-prefix VNet with four CAF-named subnets — one per tier plus jumpbox — applies WAF VNet guidance (subnet-level segmentation, NSGs per subnet) and matches tenant convention of flat single-prefix VNets (insight #9).
alternativesConsidered: Hub-spoke with separate hub VNet for jumpbox (rejected — out of scope; insight #9 favors flat)
Multiple address prefixes (rejected — not needed)
reasoning.tradeoffs: Flat topology means cross-tenant connectivity must be added later if shared services emerge.

resources.dependencyReasoning: Subnets reference per-tier NSGs which must exist first.

reasoning.whyChosen: Standard + Static is required for jumpbox VM access from the admin CIDR (Standard SKU is mandatory for current-generation IaaS scenarios; Basic SKU is retired).
alternativesConsidered: No public IP, use Azure Bastion (deferred — user requested a jumpbox VM)
Basic SKU public IP (rejected — retired)
reasoning.tradeoffs: Public IP is the attack surface; mitigated by NSG source restriction and TrustedLaunch.

reasoning.whyChosen: Jumpbox NIC bridging the management subnet and the public IP.

resources.name: vm-jump

reasoning.whyChosen: Right-sized D-series VM with TrustedLaunch (secure boot + vTPM) and OS-disk deleteOption=Detach matches tenant convention (insight #10) and WAF VM Security guidance.
alternativesConsidered: B-series burstable (rejected — TrustedLaunch + tenant convention prefers D-series)
Linux jumpbox (acceptable alternative; chose Windows Server 2022 by default for RDP-friendly admin tooling)
reasoning.tradeoffs: Single VM, single zone (per insight #2). Add a second jumpbox or Bastion if HA is required.

resources.dependencyReasoning: VM needs its NIC, the managed identity for Key Vault access, and the Key Vault-stored admin password.

resources.name: lbi-app

reasoning.whyChosen: Standard SKU internal LB with private frontend in snet-app fronts the application tier; web tier calls the LB's static private IP. HTTP health probe per WAF Load Balancer guidance (richer status than TCP-only).
alternativesConsidered: Public Load Balancer (rejected — internal east-west traffic only)
Application Gateway (rejected — out of scope for an internal L4 connector; can be added in front of web tier later for L7)
reasoning.tradeoffs: Single zone (per insight #2); set zones=['1','2','3'] on the frontend if zone-redundant HA is required. Backend pool needs ≥2 endpoints for redundancy (provided by VMSS instance counts).

resources.dependencyReasoning: Frontend IP allocates from snet-app inside the VNet.

reasoning.whyChosen: VMSS Flexible (per WAF VM guide) with 2 instances supplies the web tier behind nsg-web and originates calls into lbi-app on its private IP.
alternativesConsidered: Standalone VMs (rejected — VMSS Flexible recommended pattern)

resources.name: vmss-app

reasoning.whyChosen: App tier instances are the ILB backend pool, satisfying WAF LB guidance for ≥2 backend endpoints. Same TrustedLaunch + D-series + Detach defaults as the rest of the fleet.
alternativesConsidered: Single VM (rejected — single point of failure)

resources.dependencyReasoning: App VMSS NICs join the LB backend pool, which must exist first.

reasoning.whyChosen: Provides a tier-3 backend that the app tier connects to over the data NSG. Acts as a placeholder; can be swapped for Azure SQL / PostgreSQL Flexible Server with private endpoints when the workload solidifies.
alternativesConsidered: Azure SQL Database with private endpoint (deferred — out of scope of the explicit request)
reasoning.tradeoffs: Self-managed VM-based DB requires patching/HA effort the operator must own; PaaS DB is the recommended evolution.

overallReasoning.summary: A four-subnet VNet (mgmt/web/app/data) in westus2 enforces tier isolation through per-subnet NSGs. A Standard SKU internal Load Balancer with a static private frontend in snet-app distributes east-west traffic from the web tier into the app tier's VMSS backend pool. A jumpbox VM in snet-mgmt provides operator access locked down by NSG to a parameterized admin CIDR; the jumpbox uses a user-assigned managed identity to retrieve its admin password from Key Vault. All VMs run on D-series sizes with TrustedLaunch and OS-disk Detach to match tenant convention, and Log Analytics receives diagnostics for observability.
overallReasoning.tradeoffs: 1) User explicitly requested a jumpbox VM, so we expose a Standard public IP rather than using Azure Bastion — Bastion remains the WAF-preferred alternative for management without a public IP. 2) Single-zone defaults across all compute and the LB frontend (per insight #2) — opt into zone redundancy if HA SLO requires. 3) Key Vault publicNetworkAccess remains Enabled (per insight #3); add a private endpoint for high-isolation workloads. 4) DDoS Standard not enabled (per insight #9); add when internet-facing protection is required. 5) Data tier is a VMSS placeholder; managed PaaS data services with private endpoints are the recommended evolution. 6) Application port (8080) and DB port (1433) are placeholders to demonstrate the pattern — adjust for your actual workload. 7) Insights covering Storage Accounts (hardened-but-public pattern), Storage redundancy (LRS vs GRS/RAGRS), AKS (CNI Overlay + workload identity), App Service plans / ACR (B1/P0v3/Y1, Standard ACR), and Cognitive Services (S0 with permissive networking) are not applied because this plan contains none of those resource types — they will become relevant when the data tier is migrated to PaaS or when application workloads are added.

plan.validation: Resources form a deployable stack: RG → Log Analytics + UAI + Key Vault + per-tier NSGs → VNet (subnets reference NSGs) → Public IP → NIC (uses snet-mgmt + Public IP) → Jumpbox VM (uses NIC + UAI + KV). Internal LB depends only on the VNet (frontend in snet-app); web/app/data VMSS depend on the VNet and UAI, and the app VMSS additionally references the LB backend pool. NSG rules explicitly allow the AzureLoadBalancer service tag wherever the LB attaches, satisfying the Standard LB pairing constraint.
architecturePrinciples: Tier isolation via dedicated subnets and per-subnet NSGs
Defense-in-depth: progressively stricter NSGs from web → app → data
Secure operator access via NSG-restricted jumpbox + Key Vault-stored credentials
Internal-only east-west traffic via Standard ILB with static private frontend
Hardened compute: TrustedLaunch + Premium SSD + OS disk Detach
Observability: Log Analytics for NSG flow logs and VM Insights
Tenant-convention alignment: westus2, single-prefix VNet, single-zone defaults
